name: Amphisbaena
description: The Amphisbaena is a mythical serpent with a head at each end, originating in Greek mythology and later appearing in Roman and medieval bestiaries. Its name is derived from the Greek words 'amphis' (both ways) and 'bainein' (to go), reflecting its ability to move in either direction. This duality is a key characteristic, often interpreted as a symbol of ambiguity or the cyclical nature of life and death. Ancient accounts describe it as venomous, capable of killing with its bite, and some portray it as having eyes that glow like candles. It was believed to be born from the blood dripping from Medusa's head as Perseus flew over the Libyan desert. The Amphisbaena is typically depicted as a small, thick snake, sometimes with legs or wings, although these additions are less common in classical depictions. It is often associated with ants, which it supposedly feeds on, and its skin is said to have medicinal properties.
subtitle: A two-headed serpent that can move in either direction.
appearance: A thick, serpentine creature with a head at both ends of its body. Scales are typically brown or reddish-brown. Some depictions include small legs or rudimentary wings. Eyes are often described as glowing or fiery.
habitat: Deserts, rocky hillsides, and arid regions, particularly in North Africa according to ancient sources.
behavior: The Amphisbaena is said to be a creature of the earth, preferring to burrow underground and emerge to hunt for food. Its two heads allow it to move in either direction without turning around, making it a formidable predator and difficult to track. It is also known to shed its skin as a form of protection and renewal.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of the Amphisbaena
keyMyths.description: According to Pliny the Elder, the Amphisbaena sprung from the blood that dripped from Medusa's severed head as Perseus flew over the Libyan desert. This origin links the creature to the monstrous Gorgon and imbues it with a sense of danger and primordial power. Its birth from Medusa's blood highlights its connection to the earth and its inherent venomous nature.
keyMyths.source: Pliny the Elder, Natural History, VIII.35

keyMyths.title: The Amphisbaena's Medicinal Properties
keyMyths.description: Medieval bestiaries and folklore attribute medicinal properties to the Amphisbaena's skin. It was believed that a belt made from its skin could protect pregnant women, and other parts of the creature were used in various remedies. This belief likely stemmed from the creature's perceived connection to the earth and its ability to regenerate or shed its skin.

keyMyths.title: The Amphisbaena and Ants
keyMyths.description: Many accounts associate the Amphisbaena with ants, claiming that it feeds primarily on these insects. This association may be symbolic, representing the creature's connection to the earth and its ability to burrow underground. The ant's industrious nature could also contrast with the Amphisbaena's dual nature and ambiguous movement.

sources.author: Pliny the Elder

sources.description: A collection of anecdotes and descriptions of animals, both real and mythical. Provides insights into ancient beliefs about the Amphisbaena's behavior and diet.
